editor_settings.title: Why the Canon G7X Mark II Is Still One of the Best Cameras in 2025
editor_settings.content:
Why the Canon G7X Mark II Is Still One of the Best Cameras in 2025

A 2016 compact camera should not still be this relevant in 2025.

But the Canon G7X Mark II is not surviving because it is modern. It is surviving because it gives people something a lot of newer cameras and phones do not: photos with character, warmth, built-in flash, and just enough softness to feel good without looking cheap.

That is why this camera came back so hard.

Quick Answer

The Canon G7X Mark II is still one of the best cameras in 2025 if your priority is compact lifestyle photography, low-light social photos, travel, and that nostalgic Instagram-friendly look.

It is not one of the best if your priority is value at insane hype pricing, modern vlogging features, or pure spec-per-dollar logic.

Why People Fell Back in Love With It

The answer is simpler than camera people want it to be.

People are not chasing this camera because it wins a spreadsheet war. They are chasing it because the photos feel good.

The G7X Mark II gives you a 20.1MP 1-inch sensor, a 24-100mm equivalent lens, a bright f/1.8-2.8 aperture, and a built-in flash in a body that still feels easy to carry. That package still hits very well for:

  • night-out photos
  • restaurant and event shots
  • travel
  • lifestyle content
  • fit pics and portraits with more background than a phone usually gives you

The photos are not trying to look hyper-clinical. That is the magic. They come out softer, warmer, and less overprocessed than what a lot of people are used to seeing from phones.

The Lens Is a Huge Reason It Still Works

This is not just nostalgia talking.

That 24-100mm equivalent f/1.8-2.8 lens is a big part of why the G7X Mark II stayed relevant. It is flexible enough for wide social shots, casual portraits, and tighter everyday details, while still letting in a lot of light for a compact.

That matters because cheap trend cameras can copy the mood a little, but they usually cannot copy the whole package of brightness, zoom range, and consistency.

This is one reason the G7X Mark II still feels more serious than a random digicam, even when both are chasing the same vibe.

Why the Price Went Completely Off the Rails

Here is where things got stupid.

The G7X Mark II launched in February 2016 at about $699. By late 2025, some listings were turning up around $1,699.99.

That price spike was not because Canon secretly made it better. It happened because social media turned it into an aesthetic object.

So yes, the camera is still very good. But the market around it got irrational.

What It Still Does Better Than a Phone

An iPhone is faster. It is easier. It is always in your hand.

But the G7X Mark II still beats a phone in a few very specific ways that matter to the people buying it:

  • better natural background separation
  • a more flattering built-in flash look
  • more low-light flexibility from the bright lens
  • a result that feels more like a camera photo and less like a phone trying to fake one

That is why people keep forgiving its age.

Where the Camera Feels Old

You do need to be honest about the weak spots.

This is not a dream vlogging camera in 2025. It is usable, but old in the ways that matter.

  • video tops out at Full HD, not 4K
  • there is no external mic input
  • the flip-up screen is less convenient than a side-flip design
  • autofocus for video is fine, not confidence-inspiring
  • Canon rates it for about 265 shots with the screen on, so battery life is decent, not amazing

If you are buying it mostly to vlog, you are buying the wrong legend.

If you are buying it mostly to take pretty photos and short clips, it makes a lot more sense.

One Used-Buyer Warning You Should Not Ignore

If you are shopping used, test the flash.

Canon published an advisory on September 19, 2024 saying a very small number of G7 X Mark II units may develop a built-in flash problem from excessive pop-up use. That does not mean every used unit is risky. It does mean flash condition matters a lot more here than with some other cameras.

And since flash is one of the biggest reasons people buy this camera, that detail is not small.

Is It Actually Worth Buying in 2025?

Yes, but only at the right price.

That is the whole verdict.

At normal money, the G7X Mark II is still one of the best compact cameras for people who care about style, portability, flash photos, and low-effort results.

At absurd hype money, it stops being a smart buy and turns into a fashion tax.

If you are paying $1,200 to $1,600 just because TikTok told you this is the only camera with vibes, you are not buying well. You are buying emotionally.

The Smarter Move

If you still want the real G7X Mark II experience without paying nonsense pricing, this is the kind of listing that makes way more sense:

[product-card:canon-powershot-g7-x-mark-ii-201mp-digital-camera-7x-zoom-95-new]


That kind of gap changes the conversation completely. At a sane price, the G7X Mark II looks charming. At a hype price, it starts looking like a dare.

If even that feels too high and you only want the cheap nostalgic compact feeling, cameras like the Kodak PIXPRO FZ55 exist for much less money. Just be real about what you are giving up. The vibe may overlap a little. The lens, low-light performance, and overall flexibility do not.

Final Verdict

The Canon G7X Mark II is still one of the best cameras in 2025 because it solves a very specific problem beautifully. It makes everyday photos feel better.

It is compact, flattering, flash-friendly, and still extremely appealing for travel, social life, and lifestyle content.

But the camera itself is not the crazy part. The pricing is.

So yes, it still deserves the love. No, it does not deserve every ridiculous resale number attached to it.
